channel.title: NYT > Arts > Art & Design
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Show the Art World Loves to Hate Gets a Soul
item.description: For an unmoored time, the artists of the Whitney Biennial get personal, with an inspired discourse shaped by crisis, craft and community. Look up, and listen.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: Did Andrea Fraser’s Career Bloom Because Her Mother’s Career Died?
item.description: Andrea Fraser had long felt that she was to blame for the years her mother, Carmen de Monteflores, was overlooked. Now Carmen is 92. Can the Whitney Biennial make amends?
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Lucia Bell-Epstein
item.media:description: “If I hold this unconscious conviction that I destroyed my mother’s creativity,” Fraser said, “then this latest effort is about trying to repair things decades later.”

item.title: Fusing the Personal and the Political, With Monumental Results
item.description: Doron Langberg used to think their Israeli heritage was incidental to their art. Then the Gaza war brought questions of identity and history to the surface.
item.dc:creator: Julia Halperin and Justin J Wee
item.media:description: Doron Langberg in their Queens studio, sitting in front of their painting “Bronica Forest.”

item.title: Batman and Daredevil Artist Klaus Janson Flies Solo in Gallery Show
item.description: Klaus Janson, known for his work on Daredevil and Batman, said he hoped his new solo show could give people “a growing appreciation of what comics can do.”
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines
item.media:credit: Janice Chung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The comic book artist Klaus Janson, who for years worked alongside Frank Miller, at his show at the Philippe Labaune Gallery in Chelsea.

item.title: Russia Returns to Venice Biennale, in Latest Sign of a Cultural Comeback
item.description: By hosting a pavilion again this year, Russia continues its efforts to shed its status as a cultural and sporting pariah.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: In 2024, Russia lent its Venice Biennale pavilion to Bolivia rather than taking part in the event.

item.title: The Statues Were Mostly Men or Nude Women. So These Knitters Got to Work.
item.description: Women in Denmark, dismayed by unequal representation in public art, stitched together a protest campaign.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Abend

item.title: Yorgos Lanthimos Steps Back From the Movie Camera, and Picks Up Another One
item.description: A new exhibition in the director’s native Athens showcases his work as a photographer. “I’d like to take a break from making films,” he said. “For now, at least.”
item.dc:creator: Hester Underhill
item.media:credit: Myrto Papadopoulos for The New York Times

item.title: With Bends, Crinkles and a Cool Decor Makeover, Carol Bove Takes the Guggenheim
item.description: She extracts something new from steel, dispelling its aura of brawn. Her signature form is a rumpled ribbon of metal painted to look as soft as suede.
item.dc:creator: Deborah Solomon and Myrthe Giesbers
item.media:credit: Myrthe Giesbers for The New York Times

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in March
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Seph Rodney covers Deborah Roberts’s collages, Ursula von Rydingsvard’s wood outcroppings and Noel W Anderson’s superstars.
item.dc:creator: Seph Rodney

item.title: From 1999: Charlotte Perriand, Designer, Is Dead at 96
item.description: Le Corbusier famously told her, “We don’t embroider cushions here,” when she sought a job at his studio. Then he recognized her talent for design.
item.dc:creator: Julie V. Iovine

item.title: Tracking Down a Vase From ‘Bonjour Tristesse’
item.description: We help a reader find a prop seen in Durga Chew-Bose’s debut film “Bonjour Tristesse.”
item.dc:creator: Tom Delavan

item.title: Reclaimed Trees Get Creative Reuse With New York Heartwoods
item.description: Megan Offner finds creative and comforting ways to salvage doomed trees.
item.dc:creator: Jane Margolies
item.media:description: Megan Offner is the founder of New York Heartwoods. The circular wood products consultancy reclaims trees, providing a more meaningful and sustainable alternative to commercial-grade lumber.

item.title: Paris Again Merges as a Mecca for the Arts
item.description: Many of the galleries showing at TEFAF Maastricht are from France, a sign of the country’s growing presence in fairs and overall rising influence in art and collecting.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We are still very open in Paris,” said Nicolas Fournery, a gallerist who is showing at TEFAF for the second year. “When you compare with the United States and other countries, they are closing, but we are not in the same mood in France.”

item.title: This Small Statue in Brussels Has a Large Bladder and Lots of Outfits
item.description: Manneken Pis, the famed bronze statue of a little boy who always has to go, has an official dresser in charge of his more than 1,190 outfits.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)

item.title: How the Florists Behind TEFAF Maastricht Work Their Magic
item.description: A Dutch florist has crafted the fair’s massive, colorful arrangements since 1988. Their secrets? Early mornings, intense planning and thousands of flowers.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Morelli

item.title: Welcome to Maastricht, Where All Things French Flourish
item.description: Evident is the vast influence that France has had upon the Netherlands city, the result of its geography, history and the population’s affinity for its culture.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Sciolino and Ilvy Njiokiktjien
item.media:description: The City Hall tower in Maastricht, the southernmost city in the Netherlands.

item.title: Margrethe Odgaard’s Explorations of Color Come to TEFAF
item.description: Margrethe Odgaard, whose new textiles will be featured at TEFAF Maastricht, aims to define the distinctive hues preferred in various places, by different cultures.
item.dc:creator: Alec Scott
item.media:credit: Carsten Snejbjerg for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Danish designer and textile artist Margrethe Odgaard, who has helped create colors for many brands and products, is now using hand-dyed fabrics to create art pieces.

item.title: TEFAF Maastricht, Known for Old Art, Faces the Future
item.description: The Dutch art fair, renowned for art and antiquities, works to prove its relevance as the wider world changes around it.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn

item.title: At TEFAF Maastricht, a Paris Gallery Honors a Revolutionary French Painter
item.description: In the ’70s, Patrick Saytour and his fellow avant-gardists took their work outside the frame, exploding the notion of what painting might be.
item.dc:creator: Dale Berning Sawa

item.title: More Than TEFAF Maastricht, Other Cities Are on Display
item.description: Nearby cities in the Netherlands and Belgium will display van Gogh masterpieces, an exhibition on birds, an exploration of the evolution of beauty standards and more.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: At TEFAF, Photography Displays Add a Contemporary Flavor
item.description: As the Maastricht fair continues to update its outlook, an emphasis on photography is bringing together “the whole range of what culture can be.”
item.dc:creator: Jillian Rayfield

item.title: A Mexico City Design Gallery Hits the World Stage
item.description: AGO Projects, which specializes in contemporary work, recently turned heads by standing by an artist who had become a political lightning rod.
item.dc:creator: Ray Mark Rinaldi
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)
item.media:credit: Greta Rico for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rudy Weissenberg, left, and Rodman Primack in their Mexico City design gallery, AGO Projects. Weissenberg and Primack, longtime personal and professional partners, opened AGO six years ago.

item.title: Inside Mario Milana and Gabriella Campagna’s Endlessly Adaptable Milan Home
item.description: A couple transformed their apartment into a refuge that doubles as an ever-evolving showroom.
item.dc:creator: Kurt Soller and Danilo Scarpati

item.title: The Villa Beer in Vienna Opens as a Museum and Cultural center
item.description: The newly renovated Villa Beer in Vienna is a modernist masterpiece with a dark past.
item.dc:creator: Eve M. Kahn
item.media:credit: Andreas Meichsner for The New York Times
item.media:description: After decades of moldering, the Villa Beer property opens as a museum and cultural center, with some of time’s ravages on view.

item.title: Christian Astuguevieille, 79, Dies; Created Strange Scents and Enigmatic Objects
item.description: For Commes des Garçons, he designed improbable perfumes that conjured burning rubber and cars leaking oil. His uncanny art pieces were equally contrarian.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:credit: Marie Pierre Morel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Christian Astuguevieille at home in Paris in 1995, with his chests of knotted and tufted cord. “Altering and deforming the original use of things is important to me,” he told The New York Times in 2011. “I love to mystify.”

item.title: To Many, the Buildings Are Incredible Hulks. They’re Doomed Anyway.
item.description: Why the country is quick to tear down its modern architectural masterpieces.
item.dc:creator: Naomi Pollock

item.title: Is He the Last Man in Naples, Italy, to Paint Numbers By Hand?
item.description: The numeraio Pasquale De Stefano’s handmade market signs are a dying breed of everyday beauty in a baroque city.
item.dc:creator: Laura Rysman
item.media:credit: Pontus Berghe for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pasquale De Stefano, a number painter, or numeraio, began painting at 8, following in the footsteps of his father and grandfather.

item.title: 6 Design Products That May Be the Last of Their Kind
item.description: Many artisanal practices are hanging by a thread, but there is still a chance to enjoy them.
item.dc:creator: Diana Budds
item.media:description: Pei-Ching Hsiao started Taiwan-Lantern after learning about what is reputedly the last traditional lantern maker in her home country: a workshop in the forests of Nantou, Taiwan.

item.title: Ceramics, Once the Lifeblood of Mexican Artisans, Are Under Pressure
item.description: In the state of Michoacán, industrial agriculture and organized crime threaten a cottage industry.
item.dc:creator: Michael Snyder and César Rodríguez

item.title: Diya Vij Is New York’s Next Culture Czar
item.description: Diya Vij, the incoming commissioner of the Department of Cultural Affairs, said she wanted to make “open, accessible and sometimes radical cultural activities possible.”
item.dc:creator: Aruna D’Souza
item.media:credit: Marcus Maddox for The New York Times
item.media:description: Diya Vij, New York’s next commissioner of the Department of Cultural Affairs. Mayor Zohran Mamdani called her “a visionary and deeply thoughtful leader who understands that art is not ornamental to this city — it is essential to it.”

item.title: The Brooklyn Park Where Dozens of Snow Sculptures Bloomed
item.description: As the blizzard surged, a garden of snow sculptures arose in a Brooklyn park — a testament to New York creativity.
item.dc:creator: Michael Tyrone Delaney and Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Michael Tyrone Delaney for The New York Times

item.title: The Most Beautiful Living Rooms T Magazine Has Covered
item.description: We’re revisiting ideal spots for relaxing or hosting, from a maximalist salon in Italy to an austere seaside lounge in Japan.
item.dc:creator: T Magazine
